data.title: High-Beta Stocks Take The Lead For Equity Risk Factors In 2025
data.description:
A handful of equity risk factors are outperforming the broad stock market this year, and within the elite winner’s circle, so-called high-beta shares are leading the field, based on a set of ETFs through Wednesday’s close (Oct. 15).

The Invesco S&P 500 High Beta ETF (SPHB) is up a sizzling 27.3% year to date. The strong run this year puts the fund well ahead of the stock market overall, which has rallied 14.5% via the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY).

SPHB targets the stocks in the S&P 500 with the highest sensitivity to market movements, or beta, over the past 12 months.

The ETF is also outperforming the rest of the factor field. It’s closest competitors for year-to-date results: the momentum factor (MTUM) and micro-cap stocks (IWC), both of which are also beating the broad market in 2025. The fund’s current top-3 holdings - a trio of tech firms - account for roughly 5.9% of the portfolio: Micro Technology (MU), Tesla (TSLA) and Intel (INTC), according to Morningstar.com.

Earlier in the year, the high beta strategy (SPHB) had been trailing equities overall (SPY) by a wide margin. But SPHB’s recovery has accelerated in recent months and has become the performance leader as the trading year heads into 2025’s final 2-1/2-month stretch.

All the equity risk factors are posting gains year to date, but results vary widely. The weakest performer: small-cap value stocks (IJS), which is up a tepid 3.5% this year.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: TSMC: Unprecedented Growth Outlook For A Company Of This Scale
data.description:
Performance assessment

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (NYSE:TSM) (OTC:TSMWF) has outperformed the market since my last update on the stock, where I took some profits off the table:

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company is a long name. So that's why this is often referred to as TSMC. I will use the same acronym in this article.

Thesis

I have recently added to my position in TSM stock, as I am getting very bullish:

  1. The outlook is very bright as AI related demand is even stronger than expected just a quarter ago.
  2. There are many favorable margin developments.
  3. I like TSM's valuations here.
  4. There is no reason to fight a healthy, clear uptrend.

  5. TSM's increased investments in the U.S. mitigate tariff related risks.

The outlook is very bright, as AI-related demand is even stronger than expected just a quarter ago

The growth prospects for TSMC are tremendously strong. That's not just my opinion. This is what the CEO is telling us very directly:

…the AI demand actually continue to be very strong, it's more --more stronger than we thought 3 months ago, okay? So in today's situation, we have talked to customers and then we talk to customers' customer. So the CAGR [5-year growth CAGR] we -- previously we announced is about mid-40s. It's still -- it's a little bit better than that. We will update you probably in beginning of next year. So we have a more clear picture. Today, the number are insane.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

For me, the big highlight in that comment that gives me greater conviction for a longer-term bullish view on the stock is the fact that the 5-year growth outlook for AI chip sales is expected to be better than an already high mid-40s % growth rate. This is quite remarkable for a company with $119 billion in annual revenues (and $68 billion in high-performance computing revenues, which may correlate with AI chip sales, although I suspect some automotive chip sales should also be included in this category)!

Unsurprisingly, the high performance computing segment is the key growth contributor here. TSMC leads the market in terms of the most advanced chips and that is being reflected via a steady, relentless increase in market share (see chart above). The demand for AI compute as measured in tokens is increasing at a very fast clip at more than 30% YoY by some industry measures. And TSMC's CEO highlighted how the company's continuous innovation in next-generation processing nodes and customers' rapid uptake of that is enabling this high token usage growth:

… And let me tell you that, first, our technology continue to improve. And so our customer moving from one node to the next node so that they can handle much more tokens number in their basic fundamental calculation. So that's one thing. We progressed very well for one node into the other node, and our customer working with TSMC to continuously to improve their performance. And that's why when we say that we have about 40%, 45%, CAGR, then the token number are exponentially increased because of our customer and TSMC's technology combined that can handle much more or much efficient than before.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

Another takeaway that I think may miss the headlines due to the automotive segment's smaller 5% revenue mix is the fact that this segment has been seeing accelerated growth for the last year and a bit to now grow the second-fastest out of all segments. I believe this may give us clues on Tesla's (TSLA) robotaxi ramp as that company is a top 10 customer of TSMC.

This narrative is being reflected in an upgraded growth guidance outlook as well as FY25 revenues were bumped up to reflect ~35% YoY growth, up from the earlier 30% YoY:

This also led to a larger than usual beat on revenue guidance, which suggests that the expectations may still not be priced in completely in the stock, making a case for upside:

There are many favorable margin developments

The scale up of TSMC's 3nm (N3) node is expected to result in a marginal improvement in yields and gross margins. Now, some of this benefit will be offset by the fact that the 2nm (N2) node will see some initial gross margin dilution as that ramps up in 2026. And the good news is that longer term, the N2 node's profitability is expected to be even better than that of the N3:

N2's structural profitability is better than the N3, okay?

- CFO Jen-Chau Huang in the Q3 FY25 earnings call.

Another favorable development is that the margin dilution from overseas fabs is expected to be lower due to scale benefits and cost improvement efforts:

While the cost of overseas fabs remain higher, thanks to the company's overall larger scale, we now expect the gross margin dilution from the ramp-up of our overseas fabs to be closer to 2% in the second half of 2025. For the full year 2025, we now expect it to be between 1% to 2% as compared to 2% to 3% previously.

- CFO Jen-Chau Huang in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

These profitability boosts are leading to meaningful positive beats on gross margin guidance as well:

Overall, this makes me bullish on TSMC's gross margin profile long term. I think we can see it breach 60% next year (after the N2 ramp) and reach at least 65%+ after the current scale up cycle is complete:

I like TSM's valuations here

TSM stock is trading at a 1-yr fwd P/E of 22.6x, which corresponds to a 6.3% discount to the semiconductor comps' median multiple of 24.1x.

Another encouraging sign is that the fundamental outlook in the company as reflected in the earnings expectations index is increasing whilst the valuation multiple remains flat. This indicates an opportunity to buy a better company at the same price:

So I like find TSM's valuations rather attractive, especially given the bright outlook ahead. I think there is a clear mispricing as I cannot fathom how a company with such bright revenue growth and margin expansion prospects with a monopoly position in the highest performing chips that fuel the digital world today, can trade at a mere 22.6x 1-yr fwd P/E, lower than even the broader S&P 500's (SPY, SPX) 22.8x.

There is no reason to fight a healthy, clear uptrend

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of TSM vs SP500

TSM is in a healthy, clear uptrend vs. the S&P 500. Why fight the trend? I see no good reason to do so.

TSMC's increased investments in the U.S. mitigate tariff related risks

Whilst the U.S.-China trade war can dampen demand from Chinese customers for TSMC's chips, I suspect that the impact may be mitigated as TSMC's revenue mix profile clearly shows greater alignment toward the U.S. and the broader world:

TSMC is also increasing investments in the U.S. too as it plans to acquire a second large piece of land in Arizona to create another giga fab cluster:

In addition, we are preparing to upgrade our technologies faster to -- and to a more advanced process technologies in Arizona, given the strong AI-related demand from our customers. Furthermore, we are close to securing a second large piece of land nearby to support our current expansion plans and provide more flexibility in response to the very strong multiyear AI-related demand. Our plan will enable TSMC to scale up through an independent giga fab cluster in Arizona to support the needs of our leading-edge customers in smartphone, AI and HPC applications.

- CEO C.C. Wei in the Q3 FY25 earnings call, Author's emphasis.

Hence, I believe the impact of geopolitical, tariff and export control risk is mitigated by these actions.

Takeaway & Positioning

TSMC has annual revenues of $119 billion. Yet, even at that scale, management is clearly indicating a ~45% CAGR growth for the next 5 years. This is unprecedented. And a very strong reason to be uber-bullish on the stock, especially since the longer term gross margin outlook is also turning out more favorable.

Despite these tailwinds, TSM stock is still trading at a discount to its semiconductor sectors' median 1-yr fwd P/E multiples and below that of the S&P 500's multiple too. This may be because the market is still less confident on the geopolitical and tariff-related risks. However, my analysis of the revenue mix profile and TSMC's investments in the U.S. indicate that this risk is mitigated somewhat. Hence, I really like the stock's valuations here.

Lastly, the technical charts also show a clear bullish uptrend vs the S&P 500. Hence, I am confident that TSMC will continue to outperform.

Rating: 'Strong Outperform/Strong Buy'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PBW: Understanding The Structure And Suitability Of This Clean Energy ETF
data.description:
About The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF (NYSEARCA:PBW) is an exchange-traded index fund offered by Invesco (IVZ) that aims to track the performance of the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. According to the fund’s website:

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF is based on the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. The fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. The Index is composed of stocks of companies that are publicly traded in the United States and engaged in the business of advancement of cleaner energy and conservation. The Fund and the Index are rebalanced and reconstituted quarterly.

PBW launched on March 3, 2005, which makes it one of the first exchange-traded index funds to come out following a 2004 publication entitled “Who Cares Wins,” which is widely credited with popularizing “socially responsible investing” and Environmental, Social, and Governance investment themes. The concept revolves around ranking companies based on their environmental and social impact and then favoring those companies that rank highly in those categories. With that said, the Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF does not, strictly speaking, advertise itself as an ESG fund, nor does the index specifically state that it is weighting companies based on their social impact or governance structure. However, the index’s website does suggest that it is not strictly weighting companies based on market capitalization like most other equity indices. From the website:

We apply qualitative analysis at the Quarterly rebalancings to determine Index securities, sectors and weightings. Criteria include importance of the stock and sector to clean energy, relevance to climate change, pollution prevention, technological significance, intellectual property rights, salience to preserving biodiversity, or ecological integrity and other non-financial criteria.

Any time that an index claims to use qualitative criteria, as opposed to quantitative criteria, it is a clear sign that it is evaluating companies based on subjective criteria. As such, the actual inclusion of the various companies in this index is based on an individual’s or team’s opinions about certain non-financial factors and not necessarily the trailing performance of the stock or the overall size of the company. This is essentially the way that ESG works. The description of the index suggests that WilderHill is only interested in ranking companies based on their environmental criteria, though, not the social or governance aspects that an actual ESG index would typically include. The fact that these criteria were excluded from the website’s description does not necessarily mean that the members of the committee that designed this index are not taking social and governance factors into account when designing this index, but it does not explicitly state that on the index’s website.

How PBW Compares and Differs From Other Clean Energy Funds

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF is sponsored by Invesco, which is one of the largest providers of exchange-traded funds in the United States today. The fund has been around for almost as long as the WilderHill Clean Energy Index, which was created in 2004.

PBW is the only exchange-traded fund that aims to track the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. However, there are numerous other exchange-traded funds that invest in the clean energy sector. However, none of them is an especially large fund, as PBW is one of the largest clean energy index funds around, and it only has assets under management of $614.46 million. Here are a few of the other exchange-traded funds that are available to investors who wish to invest in clean energy:

The Virtus Duff & Phelps Clean Energy ETF is an actively managed exchange-traded fund. It is not a passive index-tracking fund like the others shown in the chart, and as such, it is not directly comparable. It is also far smaller than the others, with only $5.03 million in assets under management. All of the remaining funds are larger, although the Fidelity Clean Energy ETF has less than $100 million in assets under management.

In addition to the above funds, there are a number of exchange-traded funds that invest in specific segments of the clean energy sector. For example, a fund such as the First Trust Global Wind Energy ETF (FAN) invests specifically in wind power and eschews other types of clean energy. The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF, in contrast, includes a wider range of companies that include anything from lithium mining to solar power generation. We can see this from looking at the largest positions in the fund’s portfolio:

Lithium mining is a very different business from solar power manufacturing or generation. For example, the profitability of a lithium mining company is largely a function of the price of lithium and the costs to extract the lithium from the ground. Perhaps surprisingly, the price of crude oil is an important factor here because mining companies need to consume crude oil to power their mining equipment. Thus, a rising oil price can have a negative impact on the profits of lithium miners (and vice versa). A solar panel manufacturer is going to be adversely impacted by a rising price of silver due to the fact that a typical solar panel contains between 15 and 20 grams of silver. A company that simply generates solar electricity usually simply sells power to a large buyer under a power purchase agreement, which provides it with relatively stable revenue, although the electricity that it actually produces is dependent on the weather. In short, the companies held in PBW’s portfolio are affected by a variety of different factors, including some that a typical investor might not realize are actual factors. This will have an impact on the fund’s performance, and it is one reason why the performances of the various clean energy exchange-traded funds are different from one another.

We can see here that the various peer funds have wildly different performances in the market:

This chart shows the performance of five of the different clean energy ETFs over the five-year period that ended on October 15, 2025. For the purposes of this chart, I have removed FRNW and VCLN due to the fact that neither of those funds has a performance history for this entire five-year period (their inception dates were after October 15, 2020). Those two are also the smallest of the two funds, which might impact the willingness of investors to choose them. All five of the other peer funds are included in the chart, and we can certainly see stark differences in their performance over this particular period.

In particular, one thing that we notice is that NZAC has been much more stable than the other funds. The majority of these clean energy funds rose substantially in the months following the COVID-19 economic lockdowns, driven by a combination of incredibly low interest rates and expectations that policymakers in Washington, D.C., would look favorably upon these companies. However, renewable energy stocks eventually came under pressure as interest rates rose and threatened the economic viability of many renewable energy projects. NZAC, however, delivered a very different performance, as it never participated in the early 2021 surge, nor did it decline as far as the other funds did once interest rates started to rise. Ultimately, NZAC was the only one of these five exchange-traded funds to have delivered a positive investment return over this particular five-year period. PBW was the worst-performing fund of the peer group over this particular five-year period.

Potential investors are advised to always keep in mind that past performance is no guarantee of future results. While it is certainly true that PBW was the worst-performing fund in its peer group over the five-year period that went from October 15, 2020, through October 15, 2025, that does not mean that it will always be the worst-performing fund out of the group. In fact, over the one-year period that lasted from October 15, 2024, through October 15, 2025, PBW was by far the strongest performer out of its peer group:

This immediately tells us that factors other than regulatory policy and government backing for renewable energy projects have an impact on the performance of renewable energy stocks. After all, starting in mid-January 2025, which was about a fourth of the way into this period, President Trump replaced President Biden as leader of the United States. President Trump’s priorities are generally considered to be less favorable towards renewable energy than President Biden’s were, but the stock prices of the companies that comprise the indices that each of these funds tracks surged beginning in late April 2025, which was well into President Trump’s administration. Admittedly, it is possible that President Trump’s policy of investing the U.S. government’s money in mining companies that he deems critical for national security played a role in this. This could particularly explain why PBW surged during the first year of his administration, as that fund is more heavily invested in lithium miners – particularly Lithium Americas (LAC) – than are the other funds shown in the list. However, this does not fully explain it because ICLN also gained during the first year of the Trump administration, and that fund’s largest position is First Solar (FSLR). Investopedia speculates that the Trump administration’s guidance with respect to clean energy tax credits was less restrictive than investors feared, which helped to drive gains in solar stocks, but the actual reason for the strong performance of renewable energy stocks during the first year of the Trump administration is somewhat unclear.

Clearly, then, PBW’s performance is not going to be identical to the other clean energy ETFs. The fund has very different holdings than its peers, and this changes the overall fundamentals of the underlying portfolio. However, one thing that we do notice fairly consistently is that government policy plays a fairly significant role in the market performance of the companies held by these funds. Many governments around the world, including those in both the United States and Europe, offer things such as tax credits or direct subsidies that can improve the profitability of various projects that these companies frequently undertake. As such, investors are advised to consider the political risks before making an investment in PBW or any other renewable energy fund.

Another thing that investors should consider when purchasing shares of any exchange-traded fund, including PBW, is the liquidity of that fund. It is important that a fund have sufficient liquidity to absorb an investor’s desired trading size, or the price could move adversely against that investor. In the case of PBW, an average of 688,252 shares change hands every day. The share price as of October 15, 2025, is $35.96 per share, so this gives the fund an average of $24,749,542 of average daily trading activity. This should be sufficient to absorb the trades of most retail investors, although institutional investors might want to space their trades out in order to avoid adverse price movements. This chart shows how PBW compares with its peers in terms of liquidity:

We can see here that only PBW and ICLN have sufficient volume to absorb a fairly sizable purchase. Institutional investors will almost certainly want to favor one of these two exchange-traded funds, as will most retail investors who wish to invest more than $10,000 or so. Alternatively, investors who wish to take a larger position should probably space their trades out over a period of a few days in order to avoid adversely affecting the price. Of course, that strategy has some price risks too, as there is no guarantee that an investor would actually acquire the shares at the price that they wish. Due to the relatively small size of most clean energy funds, this is likely to be a risk with many of them.

With that said, exchange-traded funds have a mechanism in place that is designed to prevent the share price of closed-end funds from differing too much from the actual value of the assets in the fund’s portfolio. This mechanism is the use of an authorized participant, which is an entity that has the right to acquire newly created shares of the exchange-traded fund in exchange for the assets held in the fund’s portfolio. Basically, the authorized participant will purchase the same assets as the fund holds and then deliver them to the fund’s sponsor in exchange for newly created shares of the fund, which it then sells on the market to push the price down. This works pretty well whenever the share price of the fund is too high in comparison to the value of the fund’s assets. When the share price of the fund is too low relative to the value of the underlying assets, the authorized participant will purchase shares of the fund in the market and then exchange them for some of the fund’s assets, which it sells in the open market. In effect, the authorized participant is arbitraging the fund’s shares in order to keep the price in line with the net asset value.

The presence of an authorized participant does not completely offset the liquidity risks. This is because a very large trade can drive the share price of the fund either up or down more quickly than the authorized participant can act. As such, investors who wish to acquire a large position should either accumulate over a period of days (or possibly hours in the case of a large fund such as ICLN or PBW) in order to avoid having their single trade move the price.

Unique Concerns About PBW

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF aims to track the performance of the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. As mentioned in the introduction, this is something of an unusual index in that it is not weighted by market capitalization. Rather, this index uses a modified equal dollar weighting methodology. According to the website for the index (linked earlier):

The Index is calculated using a modified equal dollar weighting methodology. Component securities and weights are determined by their respective sector and size. Each Sector is assigned an aggregate weight within the index. Components less than $200 million in total market capitalization are set to one-half of a percent (0.5%). The remaining components in each Sector are equally weighted by using the Sector weightings minus the sum of the weights of less than $200 million in market capitalization. Sector weightings were initially determined by the Index Provider and are reviewed each quarter in conjunction with the scheduled quarterly review of the Index. Generally within each sector, components weighting cannot exceed four percent (4%) of the index at rebalance.

As we can see, generally speaking, each company will have the same weighting as a proportion of the index as other companies in the same sector after adjusting for fluctuations in the market. On the quarterly rebalance dates, which are the sixth business day prior to the last business day of March, June, September, and December, WilderHill changes the weightings of every stock in the index to be the same as the weightings of other companies in the same sector, assuming the other companies all have market capitalizations of at least $200 million. Smaller companies are simply assigned a weighting of 0.5% of the index. Between the quarterly rebalance dates, the weightings of each company as a proportion of the index are allowed to fluctuate depending on their relative performances in the market. It is very similar to the way that the weightings of the assets held in a retirement account might stray from their initial weightings between the dates that an investor chooses to rebalance their own portfolio.

This does not contradict the statements that I made in the introduction to this article about the index potentially being influenced by subjective factors. This is because, while the weightings of each individual company in the index may be equal in U.S. dollar terms following a rebalancing, the sector weightings themselves are not equal. The website specifically states that WilderHill weights the different sectors in the index according to its importance and technological relevance. Please refer back to the exact quote provided in the introduction for evidence of this. While the website claims that WilderHill is attempting to avoid stock picking or their views about individual companies, it is hard to see how it can apply non-financial criteria to a sector or individual company to apply weightings without the views of the index provider’s employees playing a role in the selection process. As such, there could be some risk of human error or bias here that investors need to be aware of.

For the most part, the WilderHill Clean Energy Index consists of companies that have a market capitalization of $200 million or higher, and all of these are equal-weighted with other companies in the same sector at the quarterly rebalancing. However, there can be smaller companies in the index. From the website:

To account for notable but smaller companies sometimes significant to the clean energy field, a minority of Clean Energy Index stocks may have market capitalizations between $50 million-$200 million.

It is unclear how the index provider determines whether a company is “notable” or not, and it is very possible that this is just a subjective decision made by the index provider’s employees. Other than this, however, the companies that comprise the index appear to be determined by a completely objective process. The criteria are:

  1. The company must contribute meaningfully to the advancement of clean energy technology or the deployment of clean energy generation facilities or have significant exposure to clean energy technology.
  2. The company ideally has a market capitalization of at least $200 million, but it is possible that companies with market capitalizations of $50 million to $200 million might be included if they are “notable.” No company with an average market capitalization below $50 million over the three-month period prior to the rebalancing date will be included in the index.
  3. The company must be listed on a major U.S. exchange. Unlike many other clean energy indices, this one does not include companies that do not have listings on the New York Stock Exchange or Nasdaq.
  4. The company must have sufficient trading volume over the three-month period prior to the rebalancing date to ensure that its shares are sufficiently liquid. The website does not explicitly state what these liquidity requirements are.

The first criterion shown here might be subjective, as it is generally up to the index provider to determine whether or not a company “contributes meaningfully” to clean energy technology. It could be obvious with some companies, such as a company that simply produces solar panels and nothing else. However, there are also companies such as NextEra Energy (NEE) that have a significant presence in the clean energy sector but also have other businesses that are more traditional. It is worth noting that NextEra Energy is not included in the index, but Sociedad Química y Minera de Chile (SQM) is included in the index as an energy storage company even though it has businesses other than lithium mining. Likewise, Tesla (TSLA) and Rivian Automotive (RIVN) are both included among the index’s components as power storage companies, even though both of them are primarily electric vehicle manufacturers. As such, there appears to be a certain degree of subjective criteria being applied here. The WilderHill Clean Energy Index is not purely a quantitative index, as many other indices are.

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the index had the following sector weightings:

Sector

% of Index

Renewable Energy Harvesting

13%

Energy Storage

25%

Power Delivery & Conservation

18%

Energy Conversion

21%

Greener Utilities

13%

Cleaner Fuels

10%

The weightings of these sectors change with time, as we can see by comparing the figures shown above with the sector weightings that the index had in the fourth quarter of 2024. As mentioned, the changes appear to be somewhat subjective, so it is a good idea to consult the website for the index (linked earlier) before investing money in PBW in order to be certain that the fund will have weightings appropriate to fit into an individual investor’s portfolio. As is the case with most exchange-traded index funds, PBW simply purchases the stocks in the index at the required weightings, and then the authorized participant arbitrages the shares to keep the fund’s share price in line with its net asset value. The fund changes its holdings to conform to the index on each rebalancing date.

PBW Expenses And Yield

The Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF has an expense ratio of 0.64% and a yield of 0.94%. As such, it is not really designed for investors who are in need of a high level of income. Rather, the target audience here is investors who wish to profit from the growth of the renewable energy sector in a relatively tax-efficient manner.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Invesco WilderHill Clean Energy ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that is designed to track the price and yield performance of the WilderHill Clean Energy Index. This index is somewhat unusual in that it appears to have some subjective non-financial factors regarding the companies that are included in it and the sector weightings of the index. This is one of the larger clean energy exchange-traded index funds, however, and as such has greater liquidity and superior volume to many others. Overall, it provides a reasonably tax-efficient way for investors with an interest in clean energy to gain exposure to the sector.

This article answers these three main questions about PBW:

  • Does PBW provide investors significant exposure to clean energy companies?
  • Are there unique concerns and risks associated with an investment in PBW?
  • How does PBW compare to other clean energy funds in terms of size and performance?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

SMid Cap Composite Net Performance (as of 9/30/25)*

*Sources: Conestoga, Russell Investments. Composite creation date is December 31, 2013. Please see additional important disclosures in the fully compliant GIPS presentations at the end of this commentary. Russell 2500 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2500 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values.

SMid Cap Composite- 3Q25 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga SMid Cap Composite returned -1.1% net-of-fees in the third quarter, lagging the Russell 2500 Growth Index's return of +10.7%. The narrow and "low quality" leadership which characterized the snap-back stock market rally off of the April lows intensified in the third quarter. While these dynamics are consistent with the very early stages of Small and SMid Cap outperformance cycles, in the near term they proved to be a strong headwind for our strategy's relative performance given our high-quality conservative growth discipline.

Our underexposure to both the Index's largest gainers and its high beta and low margin stocks were key to our relative underperformance. In terms of narrowness, of the 1,269 total stocks in the Russell 2500 Growth Index, the 20 best performing represented 82% of the Index's total returns in the third quarter. Looking more closely at quality, unprofitable stocks in the Index rose by 25% in the quarter, more than tripling the return of the profitable stocks. Similarly, the highest beta segment of the Index rallied over 24% in the quarter, the lowest market cap segment rose over+22%,and the lowest EBITDA margin segment rose 24%, all dramatically outperforming the overall Index. Considering these are areas of the market which our strategy and investment discipline actively steers away from, they represented major stylistic headwinds for our relative performance.

Technology stocks were the largest detractors from performance in the third quarter, as our lack of exposure to the semiconductor and technology hardware industries, combined with poor performance from our software holdings, pressured returns. To underscore, semiconductor and computer hardware rose 50% and 33%, respectively, in the quarter, building upon and exceeding their market leading gains from the second quarter. Stock selection was most challenging within the software industry, where SPS Commerce, Inc.(SPSC), Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO), and Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL) were our largest detractors, while Novanta, Inc. (NOVT) and Descartes Systems Group, Inc. (DSGX) also weighed on performance. Workiva, Inc. (WK) was a bright spot within Technology, posting a strong 27% return.

Considering the broad "low quality" and high beta leadership characteristics of the quarter, it was not a surprise to see strong returns from the Healthcare sector overall and specifically biotechnology stocks, which rose 24%. Considering these are two groups where our strategy has no exposure, this proved to be a relative return challenge for the portfolio. Four of our five positions within Health Care underperformed, led by a 20% gain from West Pharmaceutical Services, Inc. (WST), but it was not enough to keep up with the sharp gains elsewhere.

Industrials is an additional area which proved difficult in the third quarter. The strategy posted solid overall returns but simply failed to keep up with the 18.5% returns of this wide-ranging group of industries. On the positive side, Mercury Systems, Inc. (MRCY) and EVI Industries, Inc. (EVI) paced the gains for the strategy, rising 47% and 44% in the quarter, respectively. Meanwhile, Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD) rose 19.5% and Generac Holdings, Inc.(GNRC) rose 17% in the quarter. On the flip side, nine of our seventeen holdings lost ground and were led lower by CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSWI), Watsco, Inc. (WSO), and Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY), in particular.

SMid Cap Composite-3Q25 Top 5 Leaders

1. Mercury Systems, Inc. (MRCY)

MRCY is a leading designer and manufacturer of advanced electronics and subsystems for aerospace and defense applications. Shares of MRCY traded sharply higher on optimism around defense spending, strong order momentum, and easing supply chain pressures that improved delivery and margins. Investor sentiment has also improved with expectations of accelerating growth in secure, mission-critical defense electronics.

2. EVI Industries, Inc. (EVI)

EVI Industries is a distributor and consolidator of commercial laundry and dry-cleaning equipment, parts, and related services across the United States. EVI has benefited from solid demand for commercial laundry equipment and services and after years of investment we anticipate a meaningful improvement in margins following its acquisition of the highly profitable Girbau North America. The stock also benefitted from rising investor awareness after receiving its first ever sell side analyst coverage in June.

3. Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD)

ROAD provides road construction and maintenance services, including paving, grading, and site development. Infrastructure spending tailwinds from federal and state programs has fueled backlog growth and revenue visibility. The company also benefited from lower asphalt and fuel costs, which supported margin recovery, and strong project execution across its Southeastern U.S. footprint. Management's disciplined acquisition strategy has expanded its market presence into Texas, Tennessee, and Oklahoma and deepened customer relationships, enhancing long-term growth prospects.

4. Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC)

GNRC designs and manufactures backup power systems, battery storage, and clean energy solutions for residential, commercial, and industrial applications. Recurring extreme weather events and grid reliability concerns have driven heightened demand for residential and commercial standby generators. Dealer activity and channel restocking trends turned favorable, boosting near-term sales momentum. Margin recovery from cost controls and supply chain improvements also supported earnings upside.

5. West Pharmaceutical Services, Inc. (WST)

WST develops and manufactures specialized packaging, components, and drug delivery systems for injectable pharmaceuticals, biotechnology products, and healthcare applications globally. More normalized demand for biologics and injectable therapies drove strong growth in high-value packaging and delivery components. Expanding adoption of specialized containment systems supported margin expansion, while innovation in biologic drug delivery created new revenue opportunities. Investor confidence improved with consistent cash generation and a strong balance sheet enabling reinvestment and shareholder returns.

SMid Cap Composite - 3Q25 Bottom 5 Laggards

1. Casella Waste Systems, Inc. (CWST)

CWST is one of the leading regional solid waste, recycling, and resource management companies in the northeastern U.S. The company underperformed after reporting disappointing profit margin during its second quarter report. The stock weakness reflects investor frustration with the company's ability to improve margins at the assets it acquired in the mid-Atlantic region from GFL Environmental (GFL) two years ago. While we are equally frustrated, we believe management has addressed the issues and gains will come at the end of this year.

2. Q2 Holdings, Inc. (QTWO)

QTWO provides cloud-based digital banking and lending solutions to financial institutions in the U.S. and abroad. QTWO's shares were pressured by investors' concerns about information technology spending by regional banks and credit unions and company specific issues. With respect to the company specific issues, the company discussed a slightly elevated customer churn during 2Q25. The elevated churn was due to an unfavorable mix of M&A and some point solution customers not renewing its contracts. The company said that is still comfortable with sub 5% overall churn for 2025. The company is well positioned to achieve its 13% subscription revenue target for 2025 and continues to see improved profitability and cash flow.

3. Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL)

The company provides integrated information management and software solutions to public sector agencies, including courts, schools, municipalities, and state governments across the U.S. The equity market has been concerned that DOGE or federal budgetary pressures will trickle down to TYL's state and local municipalities customer's budgets. While the company has seen a select number of scattered delays or cancellations, the company has not experienced any change in public demand or purchasing behavior, and budgets have remained stable.

4. Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY)

JKHY provides core processing, digital banking, and payments technology solutions for U.S. financial institutions. The stock, along with other financial technology stocks, has been under pressure for various reasons including concerns over deposit growth and possible disruption from AI software companies. The company itself has faced some company specific growth headwinds, which we believe to be transitory, most notably outsized M+A of some of their larger customers.

5. Descartes Systems Group, Inc. (DSGX)

DSGX provides logistics and supply chain management software, including transportation management, customs compliance, routing, and mobility solutions for global customers. The stock traded lower in the quarter as global trade softness and muted shipping volumes reduced demand across its logistics network. The company initiated a 7% reduction in force to bolster margins until trade volumes normalize.

The SMid Cap Composite did not experience any new buys or complete sells, however, Conestoga added positions on three occasions and trimmed stocks on three occasions during the third quarter.

data.title: Equity Bancshares: Undervalued Growth Hidden In Plain Sight
data.description:
Thesis

Back in July, I rated Equity Bancshares, Inc. (NYSE:EQBK), based in Wichita, Kansas, which has branches in Kansas, Missouri, Oklahoma, and Arkansas, as a Buy, saying the stock was being undervalued by the market because it seemed investors were too worried about credit and deposit risks. Although this was the case, the bank had consistent loan growth, excellent capital levels, and a cautious attitude to M&A, all of which made it seem to be a good investment.

Since my report, EQBK has gone up about 3%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) has gained about 6% during the same period.

In this update, after going over Q3 earnings, which came out on Tuesday, October 14th, my research argues that Equity Bancshares remains one of the more overlooked small-cap banks in the regional space, trading below peers despite steady execution, clean credit, and clear earnings visibility.

Equity Bancshares Balances Growth with Portfolio Realignment

During the call, Equity Bancshares' management referred to Q3 as the "most transformational quarter our company has realized in its history," and the results provide credence to this perspective.

Under GAAP accounting, the company reported a loss of $29.7 million, or $1.57 per share, mainly due to a $53.4 million loss from a $482 million restructuring of its investment portfolio. In that move, the bank replaced securities earning 2.2% with cash and new securities yielding around 5%, aiming for better future returns.

After removing the effects of that portfolio change, along with $6.2 million in merger and acquisition costs and $6.2 million in loan-loss provisioning related to accounting rules, the bank’s adjusted pretax earnings came to $28.4 million, and net income was $22.4 million, or $1.17 per diluted share.

The company brought in $71.4 million in revenue, which is 31% higher than a year ago. Net interest income was $62.5 million, up $12.7 million from the previous quarter. This increase came as the company’s average earning assets grew 16% to $5.6 billion.

Its profit margin improved by 28 basis points, reaching 4.45%. CFO Navratil explained that this gain came from several factors: 13 bps from accounting adjustments on past purchases, 7 bps from fewer problem loans (nonaccruals), and 8 bps from changes in asset mix and bond investments. Without those one-time or special items, the core margin going into the next quarter would be around 4.35%.

Expenses went up because of the recent acquisition of NBC Corp. of Oklahoma, but the company actually became more efficient. Total noninterest expense was $49.1 million, or $42.9 million if you leave out M&A costs. That’s an 8.3% increase, including NBC’s numbers from before the deal. Even so, noninterest expense compared to average assets got 22 basis points better, improving to 2.80%, as the company finished integrating quickly after the late-August system conversion.

Noninterest income, excluding the repositioning, was $8.9 million, up $0.3 million from the previous period. The increase came from stronger deposit, treasury, card, mortgage, and wealth management fees, which was in line with expectations since NBC doesn’t generate much fee income.

To strengthen its liquidity and capital, the company raised $75 million through subordinated debt. By the end of the period, the company’s total risk-based capital ratio stood at 16.1%, and its TCE (tangible common equity) ratio was 9.7% at the holding company level.

Equity Bancshares Maintains Credit Strength While Expanding Scale

Credit quality stayed solid overall. When NBC was added, it brought in $7 million in nonaccrual loans and $16.7 million in classified assets (loans considered higher risk). It also added $32.8 million in purchased credit-deteriorated loans, written down 23% to reflect their lower value.

Including NBC, total nonaccrual loans reached $48.6 million, and classified assets were $82.8 million, 12.37% of regulatory capital. Without NBC’s numbers, both nonaccruals and classified assets actually fell compared with the previous quarter.

The bank wrote off about 0.10% of its loans this quarter and 0.06% so far this year. It has set aside money equal to 1.25% of total loans to cover possible losses, or 1.36% if you include purchase discounts. Management said loan performance has stayed steady across all areas, but they are keeping a close eye on quick-service restaurants, how sensitive consumers are to economic changes, and the agriculture sector.

The company’s growth engines are running well too. NBC added $665 million in loans and $808 million in deposits. Deposits that came from regular business growth, not acquisitions, went up by about $37 million, and the share of accounts that don’t earn interest improved to 22.5% of all deposits. The company made $243 million in new loans at an average rate of 7.14%, and the pipeline of potential loans (with a 75% chance of closing) stood at $475 million.

Deposit costs went up 5 basis points from the previous quarter, entirely because of NBC’s deposit mix. Management said they adjusted pricing after the Fed’s rate cut in late September, and competition hasn’t forced them to lower rates further. Meanwhile, they’re keeping an eye on recent policy changes and tariff news, but remember, since most of their business is local, management said their customers are mostly taking it in stride.

The company’s outlook was clear. CFO Navratil said they expect the net interest margin for the fourth quarter of 2025 to be between 4.4% and 4.5%. This improvement should come from gains related to changes in the bond portfolio, even as some one-time benefits from accretion and nonaccrual loans return to normal levels.

Navratil also mentioned that the company has over $3 billion in liabilities that are currently paying interest above 2%, giving it several options to manage funding costs. In addition, the company’s loan agreements have floors that provide about a 200-basis-point buffer if interest rates are cut in the future. The Frontier acquisition is still expected to close in 2025, once all approvals are received. And finally, CEO Sems reaffirmed the company’s goal to reach $5 in EPS by 2026.

How I See the Path to $5 EPS

Management’s $5 EPS goal for 2026 is based on three things I can see clearly working:

  1. A higher-yielding balance sheet now that bonds have been reset.
  2. Lower funding costs as interest rates gradually come down.
  3. Cost savings and efficiency gains from combining NBC and Frontier.

The interest rate environment is finally working in the company’s favor too. As you know, the Federal Reserve cut rates in mid-September, and Chairman Powell has signaled a more dovish, approach, suggesting that future rate hikes are unlikely or will be smaller. The Fed’s balance-sheet tightening also seems to be wrapping up, which should make it a little easier for banks to bring in deposits and reduce borrowing costs as the yield curve evens out.

Scale is the other key factor. U.S. bank mergers picked up in Q3, with 46 deals totaling $17.4 billion. Advisors say there’s been a shift toward mergers that expand a bank’s footprint and improve efficiency, which is exactly what Equity is doing with its growth strategy in Oklahoma and Nebraska.

That said, Frontier gives Equity a presence in the metro areas of Omaha and Lincoln. Once completed, management will have new ways to improve performance by optimizing the funding mix, eliminating overlapping back-office operations, and streamlining technology systems as Frontier’s operations are integrated into Equity’s platform.

How I Value Equity Bancshares Now

Looking at valuations, at $41.49 per share, EQBK's trading at about 10.06x FWD earnings, 8.20x estimated 2026 earnings, and analysts expect flat growth for the remainder of this year, followed by a 22.7% jump in 2026. So if management really hits $5 in earnings per share for 2026, then at today’s price, the stock is trading at about 8.3x earnings.

The sector’s average forward P/E's around 10.95x, and Equity’s own five-year range has been between 10x and 11x. Essentially, if management executes well, that would suggest a fair value in the low to mid-$50s, or about 25% to 40% higher than today’s price, not including dividends.

The FAST Graphs model paints a far more optimistic picture, suggesting a fair value around $115 based on a 13.24x “normal” P/E tied to operating earnings, but that definitely looks like an ultra-bullish scenario, given how many moving pieces there'll be for the remainder of this year and going into the next.

Price-to-book value gives another way to look at the stock. The shares currently trade at 1.11x their past book value and 1.10x forward book value, which is a slight discount to the sector average of 1.15x and about the same as the bank’s five-year average. Overall, for EQBK’s stock price to keep rising, the company needs to show that the bond reset, the merger with NBC, and the upcoming purchase of Frontier are creating long-term improvements in how the business performs, not just a temporary boost in profit margins.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I’d keep EQBK rated a Buy. At about 8 to 10x expected future earnings and trading at a slight discount to book value, the stock already reflects most of the credit and funding risks, but doesn’t yet factor in much of the potential upside from integrating NBC and Frontier.

data.title: Is AI A Bubble? The Dot-Com Bubble Vs. Today's AI Revolution
data.description:
AI’s growth is unlike the dot-com bubble, driven by profitable firms reinvesting in real infrastructure. Semiconductor innovation and power expansion underpin a lasting tech transformation.

The Rise of AI Companies

Artificial intelligence is driving one of the most significant infrastructure build-outs in modern history. Unlike the dot-com boom of the late 1990s, today’s AI expansion is being led by profitable global companies deploying existing cash flow. Additionally, OpenAI’s partnerships with NVIDIA (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Intel (INTC), and Oracle (ORCL) highlight how critical computing power has become as AI moves from experimentation to commercial applications. While outcomes remain uncertain, the current environment suggests that semiconductors may continue to play a central role in this evolving technological era. For investors, VanEck’s Semiconductor ETF (SMH) and VanEck’s Fabless Semiconductor ETF (SMHX) offer two distinct ways to access the ecosystem behind this compute build-out.

Artificial intelligence has become what Morningstar calls a “generational demand driver” across the semiconductor value chain. Demand is rising not just for GPUs but also for the networking, memory, and design software that support them, underscoring how pervasive this build-out has become.

AI “Bubble” vs. Dot-Com Bubble Breakdown

The late 1990s were defined by startups chasing growth with limited revenue and easy access to capital. Today’s AI expansion looks very different. The world’s largest technology companies are reinvesting substantial free cash flow into physical infrastructure because they view AI as essential to their long-term competitiveness.

Amazon (AMZN) anticipates approximately $100 billion in capital expenditures for 2025, with a significant portion allocated to cloud and AI. Microsoft (MSFT) plans to invest about $80 billion, Alphabet (GOOG) has raised its target to $85 billion, and Meta (META) anticipates investing between $66 billion and $72 billion. These are balance-sheet decisions, not speculative ventures.

Hyperscaler Planned Spend Through 2025

* Company’s anticipated capital expenditures for 2025.

Source: Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, Meta as of 2025. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities referenced herein. For illustrative purposes only. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Industry data show that semiconductor firms reinvest roughly 60% of their distributable capital in R&D and capacity expansion, a level that has doubled over the past eight years, with an annualized growth rate of nearly 10%. That suggests today’s capex is being fueled mostly by profits and innovation, not by speculative financing.

The macro backdrop is also distinct. In 2000, the Federal Reserve was tightening policy to rein in an overheating economy. As of September 2025, the Fed’s target range is 4.00 to 4.25 percent, reflecting a shift toward easing. While monetary policy could shift again, the present environment is more supportive of long-term capital investment than it was during the dot-com era.

S&P 500 Returns vs. Federal Interest Rates in the Dot-Com Bubble

The New Race for AI computing Power

OpenAI (OPENAI) sits at the center of the current AI infrastructure race. Its agreements across the semiconductor ecosystem underscore how competitive and strategic access to compute power has become.

With NVIDIA, OpenAI signed a letter of intent to deploy at least 10 gigawatts of systems beginning in 2026. NVIDIA has indicated plans to invest up to $100 billion in OpenAI as that deployment progresses. OpenAI also reached a definitive agreement with AMD to purchase 6 gigawatts of hardware over several years, including a warrant for up to 160 million AMD shares tied to deployment milestones.

Beyond GPUs, OpenAI is expanding its compute footprint through Oracle Cloud Infrastructure alongside Microsoft’s Azure. Oracle’s participation includes the development of new multi-gigawatt “Stargate” data center campuses across the United States. Meanwhile, NVIDIA and Intel have partnered to address ongoing packaging bottlenecks, with Intel expected to assist in advanced packaging and assembly.

AI Circular Deals

Partnership Announced / Updated Scope / Capacity Timing Strategic Rationale
OpenAI ↔ NVIDIA Sep-25 LOI for ≥10 GW of NVIDIA systems; up to $100B investment 3rd Qtr. 2026 start Secures GPU supply and deepens alignment
OpenAI ↔ AMD Oct-25 6 GW GPU deal; warrant for up to 160M AMD shares 3rd Qtr 2026 start Adds second source and supply diversity
OpenAI ↔ Oracle (w/ Microsoft) 2025 Azure AI extended to OCI; five “Stargate” sites (~10 GW) 2025–2027 Expands compute footprint and redundancy
NVIDIA ↔ Intel 2025 Collaboration on advanced packaging; $5B NVDA investment 2025 onward Eases packaging bottlenecks at TSMC

Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities referenced herein. For illustrative purposes only. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Forecasts from the U.S. Department of Energy and McKinsey indicate that cloud and hyperscaler capital spending could exceed $450 billion by 2027, up from roughly $150 billion in 2023. Those figures illustrate the sheer scale of infrastructure investment now underway to support AI demand.

The Bottlenecks Ahead

Despite this wave of spending, capacity remains limited in key areas. TSMC’s advanced packaging process, for example, is expected to reach around 75,000 wafers per month in 2025, yet that may still fall short of global demand. The supply of high-bandwidth memory (HBM) is also tight, as SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF), and Micron (MU) work to improve yields on the latest HBM3E technology.

According to data from Gartner, HBM represented roughly 17–20% of DRAM (Dynamic Random Access Memory) demand in 2024 and could reach nearly 50% by 2029, with suppliers expected to more than double capacity in 2025. Advanced packaging is also emerging as a key enabler of AI performance, expected to grow around 8% annually this decade as chip designers adopt chiplet and 3D stacking techniques.

Power and cooling constraints present another challenge. The development of multi-gigawatt data center campuses depends on local grid expansion, permitting, and environmental planning—factors that can delay or cap the pace of deployment.

From Build-Out to Scale-Out

For investors, these developments highlight two complementary parts of the semiconductor landscape.

SMH represents the companies responsible for building the physical backbone of AI. It tracks the MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index, which is composed of large, liquid semiconductor and equipment companies that generate at least half of their revenue from the industry. These are the companies that produce the chips, tools, and systems that enable the world’s compute expansion.

SMHX captures the fabless innovators driving AI’s scale-out. It tracks the MarketVector US Listed Fabless Semiconductor Index, which includes chip designers creating architectures and connectivity solutions aimed at improving efficiency and overcoming memory and power constraints.

Beyond manufacturing, design-led innovation remains critical. Fabless chip designers, EDA software firms, and IP licensors have delivered some of the strongest growth and margin expansion in recent years - a dynamic that directly connects to SMHX’s focus on the fabless, innovation-driven segment of the market.

Designers Have Best Growth; Designers, TSMC (TSM), Equipment Have Great Margins

Historical revenue CAGR and average operating margins from 2018 to 2014.

Source: Morningstar as of 2025. Not intended as a recommendation to buy or sell any securities referenced herein. For illustrative purposes only. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Together, these two funds provide diversified exposure across the semiconductor value chain. SMH reflects the structural build-out of manufacturing and infrastructure, while SMHX focuses on the design-side innovation that helps AI systems run faster and more efficiently.

Bubble or Structural Transformation in Motion?

Every major technological shift begins with years of foundational investment. Railroads, electrification, and the rise of the internet all followed this path, with infrastructure build-outs paving the way for future productivity gains. The current expansion of AI compute infrastructure may represent a similar phase in technological progress.

According to projections from the U.S. Department of Energy and McKinsey, AI-driven data center power demand is compounding at 22% to 33% annually, far outpacing historical energy-use growth. This reflects how the AI revolution extends well beyond semiconductors - it is transforming the physical infrastructure of compute itself.

While no one can predict how this will ultimately unfold, the magnitude and composition of current spending point to a longer-term structural trend rather than short-lived speculation. The semiconductor industry sits at the center of this transformation.

SMH offers a way to participate in the broader infrastructure build-out, while SMHX focuses on enabling designers and innovators to scale AI. Together, they provide diversified access to the companies shaping what may prove to be one of the most important technology transitions of our lifetime.
