data.title: Energy Transfer: Bottom-Fishing The Midstream Sector
data.description:
Introduction

Greetings, midstream aficionados and energy equity bargain hunters all over the world. A quick search of the sector reveals a Grouper waiting to be caught. Units of Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) are marked down at a steep discount to other members of the Big Cap cohort. Units of ET have just been pummeled following the January warm-up and subsequent fallout from Liberation Day in the energy sector generally.

By comparison, many members of the Midstream Big Cap cohort have done pretty well, as the graph below illustrates. YoY units of ET have round-tripped to the bottom, after an early, nearly sector-leading 28.5% gain in late January-25. They aren't alone in this swoon, but fall far behind two market laggards we have highlighted in recent times, Enterprise Products Partners, (EPD) and TC Energy, (TRP). If you missed either article, EPD can be accessed here, and TRP is available here.

As always, the question before us is should we stoop to pick up that 8.1% yield, wait for it to fall farther, or avoid it all-together. Seeking Alpha analysts are mostly bullish at current levels, while the Street is champing at the bit to load up on this beleaguered energy giant.

I've always been a bargain hunter, sometimes driving on fumes in search of a few cents off on a gas fill-up. I had some near misses back in the day, if I am honest, in the nearly trackless desert wildernesses of far west Texas and the Oklahoma panhandle. I spent a lot of my time in those parts, checking rigs, before going offshore in the early 90s. In search of a better deal, I would nervously watch the gas gauge drop as I nervously scanned the horizon for a "Fill Up Here," sign. Younger readers may be surprised to learn that Google Maps and Waze had yet to be invented, and if you were lost and low on gas in those neighborhoods, you could have a bad day in the oilfield. Luck was always with me on that score, and I never had to push the car to the next one. But, it was close more than once, as the car bucked toward the pump.

Ok, I'm getting too chatty here. The relevance to our topic of all this verbiage is my obsession with getting the best price. In this case, with the decline we have seen, it's fair to ask if ET is cheap for a reason. In which case, we definitely want in at the bottom, or as near to it as we can get.

ET rained on their own parade with cautionary guidance for the upcoming quarter, noting weakness in the Bakken, and worries about a resurgence in the dry gas business. At the same time, they noted optimism about the Desert Southwest gas pipeline being fully contracted after the open season.

So, some pros and cons to start. Let's dig in.

The Macro For Midstream

I pulled this quote from the EPD article as it applies to ET as well-

We always like to look at the backdrop, or macro environment, in which an industry operates before diving into the company specifics. I don't think the investment macro case for this sector has ever been stronger. Whether you look at it from a political—the new administration is very friendly toward the industry—domestic domestic requirements-the nascent data center business and even solar farms need the dispatchable power that gas provides, and it gets where it's going via pipelines—or export opportunities-via LNG shipments to Asia and the EU.

A note put out in April by Invesco is very bullish on the sector. So the macro is stronger now than it's ever been in Midstream.

The Thesis For ET

The ET "swoon," shall we call it, appears to have plateaued just as we approach winter heating season. With all the new LNG demand +5BCF/D year over year, and the power burn from Data Center start-ups, heading into peak season, has us wondering if we haven't seen a bottom over the near term. Is it time to go with the Street and load up on ET?

ET, as all know, is the largest quasi-utility bulk oil gas transporter in the country, with over 140K miles of pipe installed. I say quasi-utility because, even though they are private company, as their infrastructure crosses state lines, they are heavily regulated by the Federal Government.

Like other Big Cap Midstreamer's we have reviewed recently, ET also participates in a number of businesses related to the storage and intermediate processing of gas and oil. ET calls it Wellhead to Water.

We should also note their massive infrastructure position in West Texas, Southwest Texas, and New Mexico, where the bulk of drilling activity remains. New drills keep the existing pipes full and make the case for expansion.

With $5.0 bn in growth capex, the bulk of which is split between the gassy Permian Basin, NGL infrastructure upgrades and expansions, and the Interstate Hugh Brinson pipeline, the company has seeded substantial revenue enhancement for the near term.

ET pays a currently 8+% yielding distribution and has a track record of incremental increases annually for at least ten years.

Two Catalysts For Growth

What you don't see a lot of when you like at pipeline maps of the U.S. is pipes headed west. Once you leave the plains of Texas, New Mexico, and Oklahoma, the terrain becomes very mountainous. I suppose it was just easier to build them east, and coal by rail was the dominant electric fuel for a hundred years. In 2029, the Desert Southwest expansion of the Transwestern pipeline will be complete. 1.5 BCF/D of Permian natural gas will start arriving in Phoenix, and probably just in time.

The Hugh Brinson new build pipeline from the Permian to eastern hubs will enter service late next year. If the Data Center gambit pans out as it appears it will, ET will reap the rewards of supplying these applications.

Q2 Results And Guidance

Energy Transfer reported net income attributable to partners for the three months ended June 30, 2025 of $1.16 billion compared to $1.31 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2024. For the three months ended June 30, 2025, net income per common unit (basic) was $0.32.

Adjusted EBITDA for the three months ended June 30, 2025 was $3.87 billion compared to $3.76 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2024.

Distributable Cash Flow attributable to partners, as adjusted, for the three months ended June 30, 2025 was $1.96 billion compared to $2.04 billion for the three months ended June 30, 2024.

Growth capital expenditures in the second quarter of 2025 were $1.04 billion, while maintenance capital expenditures were $253 million.

ET reports Q3 on Nov 5th, and EPS is expected to come in at $0.28 per unit and revenues at $21.79 bn. That would be a nice bump from Q2's $18.8 bn, and the prior year's $20.3 bn.

Company Filings

Risks

A couple of things come to mind in this area. First, there is the delay that's occurred with Lake Charles LNG thanks to the waffling of the last administration. The DOE has granted them another a couple of years to begin shipping product, but there are questions as to whether they are missing the moment. Concerns exist about the potential for oversupply in the LNG space from late 2027. With Lake Charles not being operational until the early 2030s, it's fair to ask questions.

AI is part of a trifecta of rationale-AI, LNG exports, and backup for Solar, that every company in the midstream or downstream space is hawking as the basis for billions of infrastructure capex outlay. A recent WSJ article pointed out that no one is making any money in this endeavor yet. And no one knows when or if anyone ever will. Is the AI mania just that? Mania? I am old enough to remember the hype about the fiber optic cable, internet buildout of the late 1990s. It all came crashing down a few years later, and a lot of "billion-dollar" companies went bankrupt, or nearly did. It was nearly a "1929" moment, and took a decade from which to recover. Is AI headed for a cliff?

I don't think so. It's different this time. AI is being incorporated into everyday life at a rate and on a scale that the internet buildout never came close to achieving. It's a risk, though, that if demand doesn't continue to rise, all this capex, it could punch a hole in ET's future earnings.

Your Takeaway

As we have noted, ET trades at a substantial discount to its big-cap peer group. I think while we acknowledge that risks are on the radar to its business model, they apply across the board. I think ET rates a buy for its well-covered 8+% yield, and the prospects of continued increases coming from growth projects.

If ET beats the street when earnings are released on Nov 5th, shares should get a bump. ET is an MLP, meaning it issues a K-1. You should consult with an investment advisor-which I am not—about the suitability of this type of equity in your portfolio.

data.title: Conestoga Mid Cap Composite Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance & Attribution

The Russell Midcap Growth Index’s large rally of 18.2% in the second quarter was followed by a more muted gain of 2.8% in the third quarter of 2025. Since the April 8th market low, the benchmark has risen 37.0%, driven largely by lower-quality stocks that have historically led during the early stages of a recovery. Leadership has also narrowed, with the top ten contributors to Index returns in the third quarter accounting for 130% of the overall Index returns. Most of these names fall outside of our investable universe and many of them are AI infrastructure related stocks. In comparison, the Conestoga Mid Cap Growth Composite returned -3.2% net-of-fees in the third quarter, as our emphasis on high-quality, profitable companies couldn’t keep pace with the market’s risk-on leadership.

The Technology sector proved most challenging and was the largest detractor from relative returns in the quarter. Negative stock selection effects and our large overweight to the software industry, coupled with our lack of exposure to the high-flying semiconductor industry was a drag on performance. Semis and other “hard tech” industries have significantly outperformed software companies in recent months as the surge in demand for artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure, high-performance computing, and cloud data centers has created a powerful tailwind for chipmakers and capital equipment providers. By contrast, software has faced headwinds from slower enterprise IT budgets, elongated sales cycles, and cautious spending by small and mid-sized businesses. Many software companies have seen multiple compression as growth has moderated. Our positions in Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT), Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL), and Roper Technologies, Inc. (ROP) were the biggest laggards in the software space.

Within Industrials, results were mixed. Verisk Analytics, Inc. (VRSK) was the largest detractor in the portfolio during the quarter, as insurance carriers moderated spending on data and analytics solutions amid macro and underwriting uncertainty. Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY) also underperformed as subscription growth slowed, and investors grew concerned about rising implementation costs. Our positions in Generac Corp. (GNRC) and Xylem, Inc. (XYL) partially offset the losses in Industrials. GNRC benefited from dealer activity and channel restocking trends that turned favorable, boosting near-term sales momentum. XYL continued to perform well on robust demand for water infrastructure and utility solutions, supported by federal and state infrastructure funding.

Positively, sector allocation decisions added modestly to performance. Our relative underweights in Financials, Energy, and Consumer Staples proved beneficial, as all three sectors underperformed the benchmark. In addition, positive stock selection effects within the Health Care sector provided a boost to returns. West Pharmaceutical Services, Inc. (WST) and Idexx Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX) were the largest contributors during the quarter.

Top 5 Leaders

1. West Pharmaceuticals Services, Inc. (WST)

WST develops and manufactures specialized packaging, components, and drug delivery systems for injectable pharmaceuticals, biotechnology products, and healthcare applications globally. Robust demand for biologics and injectable therapies drove strong growth in high-value packaging and delivery components. Expanding adoption of specialized containment systems supported margin expansion, while innovation in biologic drug delivery created new revenue opportunities. Investor confidence improved with consistent cash generation and a strong balance sheet enabling reinvestment and shareholder returns.

2. IDEXX Laboratories, Inc. (IDXX)

IDXX is the industry leader in providing instruments (and consumables) used in diagnostics, detection, and information systems for veterinary, food, and water testing applications. Earnings for the quarter were strong as organic revenue growth accelerated, despite weak veterinarian visits, and the company saw rapid adoption of their latest premium instrument.

3. AAON, Inc. (AAON)

AAON designs and manufactures highly engineered, energy-efficient commercial and industrial HVAC systems. AAON’s 2Q results again initially disappointed investors but investors ultimately viewed it as a clearing event. The company’s year-to-date results have been adversely impacted by sluggish demand in its core rooftop HVAC business, manufacturing inefficiencies caused by an ERP implementation and expansion expenses of its Memphis, TN manufacturing facility. The stock’s positive reaction is a function of the company’s continued strong backlog of $1.1 billion, the substantial growth of the BASX business which manufactures cooling equipment for the data center market and a stabilization of the manufacturing operations as ERP implementation has been less of a drag on output.

4. Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC)

GNRC designs and manufactures backup power systems, battery storage, and clean energy solutions for residential, commercial, and industrial applications. Recurring extreme weather events and grid reliability concerns have driven heightened demand for residential and commercial standby generators. Dealer activity and channel restocking trends turned favorable, boosting near-term sales momentum. Margin recovery from cost controls and supply chain improvements also supported earnings upside.

5. Xylem, Inc. (XYL)

XYL designs and manufactures water technology equipment and solutions, including pumps, treatment systems, analytics, and smart infrastructure technology. The stock has performed well on robust demand for water infrastructure and utility solutions. Strong order momentum in wastewater, analytics, and smart metering segments lifted backlog and revenue visibility. Margin improvement from cost synergies following the Evoqua acquisition further enhanced earnings.

Bottom 5 Laggards

1. Verisk Analytics, Inc. (VRSK)

VRSK provides data analytics and decision-support solutions, primarily for the insurance industry. VRSK’s stock retreated significantly in the 3Q after a very solid 1H25. The company reported a very solid 2Q with organic revenue growth of 7.9%. Verisk’s second-quarter results came in ahead of “Street” expectations across revenue, adjusted EBITDA, adjusted EBITDA margin, and adjusted EPS. Alongside its earnings release the company announced the $2.5 billion acquisition of AccuLynx, a SAAS-based software business. Investors paused on the 15x revenue multiple the company paid for this asset. The stock’s decline also reflects moderating organic growth expectations for the back half of the year.

2. Gartner, Inc. (IT)

IT is a leading research and advisory firm that provides insights, consulting, and events to help business and technology leaders make informed strategic decisions. Shares have lagged as contract value for its IT research services continues to be weak. In addition, there is a growing narrative that the company’s business model is under threat from AI deep research tools, an argument that is difficult to disprove until contract value reaccelerates.

3. Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT)

FTNT is the worldwide market share leader in network security firewalls (by units). During the quarter, the company posted solid results, but also said they were much further along the refresh cycle than investors were expecting. This resulted in significant share pressure as revenue growth estimates for 2026 and 2027 were revised lower.

4. Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY)

JKHY provides core processing, digital banking, and payments technology solutions for U.S. financial institutions. The stock, along with other financial technology stocks, have been under pressure for various reasons including concerns over deposit growth and possible disruption from AI software companies. The company itself has faced some company specific growth headwinds, which we believe to be transitory, most notably outsized M+A of some of their larger customers.

5. Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL)

The company provides integrated information management and software solutions to public sector agencies, including courts, schools, municipalities, and state governments across the U.S. The equity market has been concerned that DOGE or federal budgetary pressures will trickle down to TYL’s state and local municipalities customer’s budgets. While the company has seen a select number of scattered delays or cancellations, the company has not experienced any change in public demand or purchasing behavior, and budgets have remained stable.

3Q25 Buys*

None.

3Q25 Sells*

1. FactSet Research Systems, Inc. (FDS)

FDS delivers financial data, analytics, and software to investment professionals worldwide. Organic growth has slowed amid budget constraints at asset managers and intensifying competition from Bloomberg, S&P, and new fintech entrants. Margin pressures from rising technology and content costs further challenge earnings momentum. While the company remains a high-quality franchise, its premium valuation leaves little cushion for execution risks.

Conestoga added to positions on one occasion and did not make any trims during the third quarter.

*Portfolio holdings shown above experienced material activity during the quarter

Holdings of each separately managed account in a composite will differ from the index. An investor may not invest directly in an index. data.title: Arista Networks: Buoyed By Broadcom As OpenAI's Mega Deals Continue
data.description:
I previously covered Arista Networks Inc. (NYSE:ANET) (NEOE:ANET:CA) in August 2025, discussing how the rich monetization of its new Ethernet offerings had been highly promising, as observed in the double-beat FQ2'25 performance and the raised FY2025 guidance.

Despite the growing purchase commitments and the hyperscalers' raised capex guidance underscoring the durability of the multi-year cloud spending trends, I had believed that the stock's rally had occurred overly fast and furious then, with the decelerating growth profile and the overly expensive valuations also triggering a minimal margin of safety.

In this article, I shall discuss why ANET may continue benefiting from the multi-year cloud supercycle, thanks to the growing AI networking demand, the highly strategic partnership with AVGO, and the billion-dollars' worth of OpenAI mega deals.

While ANET may very well outperform in the upcoming FQ3'25 earnings call and potentially offer raised FY2025 guidance, it is uncertain how long the upward momentum may last. Given the premium valuations and the potential tariff impacts on the wider semiconductor sector from Q3'25 onwards, I continue to rate the stock as a Hold.

ANET's Rally Occurred Overly Fast & Furious as the AI Mania Continues

ANET 1Y Stock Price

Since that downgraded rating to a Hold, ANET has mostly traded sideways at +7%, with the wider market at +4.1%, compared to other AI-focused stocks, Broadcom (AVGO) at +15.5%, NVIDIA (NVDA) at +3.4%, and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) at +25.6% over the same time period.

Much of the latter three's tailwinds are attributed to their ability to participate in OpenAI's mega deals, based on the latest announcement from:

  • NVDA, for the investment of $100B in OpenAI, with the latter expected to deploy at least 10GW of AI data centers with Nvidia systems,
  • AMD, with the issuance of a warrant for up to 160M shares to OpenAI, worth the equivalent of $34.62B by the time of writing, for the deployment of 6GW of AMD's AI GPUs, and finally,
  • AVGO, for the collaboration for 10GW of custom AI accelerators and server racks "scaled entirely with Ethernet and other connectivity solutions from Broadcom."

While much of these announcements do not directly impact ANET as a supplier, the last collaboration between OpenAI and AVGO is important in my opinion.

This is because ANET and AVGO are networking partners, where the former's networking switches utilize the latter's ethernet adaptors/chips

This also explains why AVGO's robust FQ3'25 AI semiconductor revenue growth to $5.2B (+18.1% QoQ/+48.5% YoY) has contributed to ANET's growing high-speed data-center switching dollar share to 33.4% in Q4'24 (+3.5 points YoY), as last reported by Crehan Research with the latest Q2'25 data yet available.

With AVGO already hinting at excellent AI revenue growth from ~$19.9B in FY2025 to approximately $31B in FY2026 (+55% YoY), if not more, I believe that ANET is likely to benefit as a networking key supplier moving forward.

This has not even taken into account AVGO's new deal with OpenAI, where the deployment of the 10GW racks of AI accelerator and network systems is expected to commence from H2'26 through 2029, with it likely to further drive ANET's data center switching dollar share expansion.

Readers must also note the tailwinds arising from ANET's two key customers' higher capex trends ahead, namely Microsoft (MSFT) and Meta (META), both of which comprise 20% (+2 points YoY) and 15% (-6 points YoY) of the former's revenues in FY2024.

For example, MSFT has already highlighted its plans to "mainly use its own chips in its data centers in the future ... [to] reduce its reliance on major players like Nvidia and AMD"—a development that implies a greater reliance on ANET's switching products and away from NVDA's Spectrum-X Ethernet switches.

With MSFT already guiding FQ1'26 spending of over $30B (+75.6% QoQ/+101% YoY) and reporting $368B of contracted backlog across cloud/Azure offerings (+33.8% YoY), it is apparent that its long-term hyperscaler prospects remain extremely bright, with the higher capex trends likely to benefit ANET as a key infrastructure supplier.

A similar trend has been observed in META, at which the social media giant expects to spend $69B in data center capex in FY2025 (+85.2% YoY) while hinting at intensified cloud capex at over $100B (+44.9% YoY) in FY2026, as they also announce the world's first 1GW Prometheus cluster and the 5GW Hyperion (to be completed over the next few years).

With hundreds of billions of capex likely to be a new normal moving forward, I believe that META's data center investments are likely to integrate switches from different suppliers, ANET's and NVDA's, despite the recent networking partnership announcement between META and NVDA.

This is especially since META's custom AI accelerators are designed with AVGO, with it lending further credence to ANET's strategic position as AVGO's key networking partner.

Robust Cloud Spending Trends

These reasons are also why ANET may enjoy a rich growth prospect arising from the expanding generative AI networking equipment market size from $8B in 2023 to $34B in 2028 at an accelerated CAGR of +33.5%, with it likely to trigger the expansion in its AI networking market share moving forward.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

As a result of the growing AI monetization opportunities, which have been well supported by the consecutive double-beat performances over the past sixteen quarters, the raised FY2025 guidance, and the notably higher purchase commitments, I can understand why the consensus have already raised their forward estimates.

This is with ANET expected to report an accelerated top/bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +22%/+20.8% through FY2027, compared to the prior estimates of +20.7%/+14.4% a year ago, while building upon the 10Y historical growth profile at +28.1%/+39.4%, respectively, with it underscoring its ability to monetize its networking offerings during the multi-year cloud super cycle.

ANET Valuations

Thanks to these market developments, it appears that the market has already priced in another outperforming FQ3'25 earnings call and a potentially raised FY2025 guidance, with the same observed in ANET's upgraded FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 54.88x, up from the 1Y mean of 41.88x, the 5Y mean of 35.61x, and the 10Y mean of 31.91x.

Despite the raised consensus forward estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +20.8%, it goes without saying that the stock is increasingly expensive at 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.63x, compared to the 5Y mean of 2x and the 10Y mean of 0.80x.

When compared to its direct networking peers, including:

  • AVGO at FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 48.17x/FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.62x,
  • NVDA at 40.67x/1.13x, and
  • Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) at 16.80x/2.69x, respectively,

it is apparent that ANET is overly expensive after being buoyed by the multi-billion OpenAI deals, with it presenting great risks entering the Q3'25 earnings call.

ANET Stock Remains a Hold Here

ANET 5Y Stock Price

For now, the AI bulls have well defended ANET's upward momentum by +105.3% since the April 2025 bottom, with the stock consistently running away from the 50/100/200-day moving averages while charting a new peak in the $162 range.

Based on the consensus consistently raised FY2027 adj. EPS estimates of $4.00 and the 1Y P/E mean of 41.88x (nearer to the peer group mean of ~40.13x), it is apparent that the stock has already pulled forward much of its upside potential to my base-case long-term price target of $167.50.

Even if there remains an excellent upside potential of +48.8% to my bull-case long-term price target of $219.50, it is contingent on ANET sustaining its rather premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations to 54.88x - a highly speculative conjecture attributed to the premium over its peers and the ongoing AI bubble.

ANET's Technical Indicators

This is especially since ANET's technical indicators appear mixed at current levels, as observed in the moderating RSI and the inflated trading volumes nearing prior 2024/2025 peaks, with it uncertain if the stock may sustain its upward momentum entering the Q3'25 earnings season.

If anything, traders may potentially take their gains off the table at current heights, worsened by the volatility arising from aggressive short sellers, as observed in the notably higher short interest volumes by +15.9% on a YoY basis (post stock split).

As a result of the premium valuations, the pulled-forward upside potential, and the uncertainties arising from the potential tariff impacts on the wider semiconductor sector from Q3'25 onwards, I prefer to maintain my Hold rating for the ANET stock here.

Given the elevated dollar cost at current levels, I do not recommend anyone chase this rally over the cliff.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Ramping Up A New Billion-Dollar Segment (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Hims & Hers Health, Inc. (NYSE:HIMS) in February, I downgraded it to Hold. Despite much volatility in the stock price since then, the stock has been underperforming the S&P 500 (SP500) during this period of time.

The company announced the launch of its Menopause and Perimenopause specialty yesterday, sending the stock up 16%. This specialty is part of its “Hers” segment, which currently consists of half a million subscribers. Although the company does not break out its revenues by “Hims” and “Hers” separately, management estimates that the expansion of this specialty will enable them to surpass $1B in revenue for “Hers” in 2026, which would roughly amount to 36% of total estimated revenue.

The way I see it, the announcement is very timely, as the stock has been range-bound for a long period of time, especially after the termination of its partnership with Novo Nordisk (NVO). Although the management heavily emphasized building a full-spectrum, vertically integrated, personalized healthcare platform, the company saw its revenues in Q2 FY 2025 decline sequentially for the first time since going public, driven by softness in demand for its compounded GLP-1.

However, the growth rate in personalized treatments remained strong. In the meantime, Hims & Hers is taking a long-term view where it is making strategic capital investments and acquisitions to expand into new specialties as well as across geographies in the coming years.

Plus, what caught my attention was the magnitude of difference in revenue estimates between consensus and management’s FY 2030 projections. Coupled with a short float of 35%, the timing of the new specialty launch in “Hers” could just provide the stock the much-needed re-rating that I believe it deserves.

As a result, I will upgrade my rating on the company to a Buy with an updated price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Expanding “Hers” to Achieve $1B In Revenue in 2026

Hims & Hers announced the launch of a Menopause and Perimenopause specialty in women’s health yesterday. At the moment, “Hers” serves over half a million subscribers, and by expanding their offerings in Menopause & Perimenopause, Hers is now on track to surpass $1B in annual revenue in 2026.

To put things in perspective, the company has a total of 2.4M subscribers that were reported on their Q2 FY 2025 earnings. This means that their share of women subscribers currently stands at roughly 20.8%. While the company does not break their revenue by “hims” and “hers” (men and women separately), consensus estimates currently point to a total revenue of $2.79B by FY 2026. Therefore, assuming that Hers's revenue surpasses $1B, it will account for 36% of Total revenue by FY 2026.

The announcement of the launch is very timely as the company tries to shield its revenue from GLP-1-related volatility by launching new offerings in hormone health, lab testing, and longevity-focused care and positioning itself as a “personalized health and wellness platform.”

The Timing Couldn’t Have Been Better

In the previous shareholder letter, the management discussed at length their vision of building a “next-generation” healthcare platform by placing customers at the center of every decision.

In fact, I believe that the following slide from their earnings presentation captures their value proposition quite accurately, as they move beyond simply connecting customers to providers to delivering truly personalized care.

Think of it as the Hims & Hers flywheel effect, where more subscribers lead to more proprietary data that allows providers to make more informed decisions, leading to superior customer experience, which in turn fuels even more subscribers onto the platform.

Yet, the stock has remained range-bound for quite a while, especially after the termination of its partnership with Novo Nordisk.

While I am not going to get into the details of why the partnership was terminated in the post, it hurt investor sentiment when Hims & Hers reported its first sequential revenue decline in Q2 FY 2025 since it went public.

During the quarter, Hims & Hers reported a year-over-year revenue growth of 73% to $545M, missing estimates by 1.3%. One of the main reasons is tied to its compounded GLP-1 revenue, which fell 17.39% sequentially to $190M, accounting for 34.8% of Total revenue.

However, if we look at the details, the underlying fundamentals at Hims & Hers still remain quite strong. The company ended the quarter with 2.4M subscribers, growing 31% YoY. Nearly 70% of the new subscribers who joined the platform are utilizing a personalized treatment, while their total count of subscribers on a personalized treatment grew 89% YoY, with average revenue per subscriber growing 30% YoY to $74.

In the meantime, the company plans to add lab testing and expand into longevity-focused care in 2026, along with spearheading its growth opportunities internationally with its recent acquisition of a leading digital healthcare provider called ZAVA Global. The company estimates at least $50M in revenue from ZAVA through the remainder of FY 2025 at a positive adjusted EBITDA margin as they expand their presence in the UK, Germany, France, and Ireland.

In my earlier posts on Hims & Hers, I had often said that the company is more than just a compounded GLP-1 provider but more of a vertically integrated healthcare infrastructure. While I strongly believe that to be the case as of this writing, the truth is short-term investor sentiment seems to be heavily correlated to the volatility of the news surrounding GLP-1s. Last month, Hims & Hers shares declined when the US FDA issued a warning letter over its marketing of compounded semaglutide, the weight loss drug marketed under the brand name Wegovy by Novo Nordisk.

While it is not uncommon for pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies to receive warning letters from the FDA for various reasons, it has certainly not helped Hims & Hers stock for several months now.

However, with the company launching new offerings in Hims last month and expanding its specialty with menopause and perimenopause in “Hers,” it could give investors the required confidence in the management’s vision in building a full-spectrum healthcare platform and reaching its long-term targets that we will discuss in the next section.

Some Short-Term Patience Is Required

Aside from revenues sequentially declining in Q2, one of the other reasons investor sentiment was dampened was because of its mixed picture in profitability.

While the adjusted EBITDA margin grew 300 basis points on a year-over-year basis to 15%, their operating and free cash flow turned negative, as can be seen below.

This was driven by working capital investments and an increase in capex to deliver superior personalization and broader capabilities of their verticalized pharmacy structure.

However, the way I see it, the company reiterated their long-term 2030 revenue and adjusted EBITDA targets of $6.5B and $1.3B, respectively. This would require the company to grow their revenues and adjusted EBITDA at a CAGR of 22.5% and 33%, respectively, over the next five years based on management’s updated FY 2025 guidance.

However, markets are grossly underestimating management’s FY 2030 projections. Current consensus estimates point to Hims & Hers delivering a total revenue of $4.21B by FY 2030, which is 35% below management’s projections.

In the image above, you can also see that the company has received a storm of downward revisions to their forward revenue growth estimates on a 3M basis, which coincides with the timing of the cancellation of their Novo Nordisk partnership.

With the magnitude of difference between consensus estimates and management projections for FY 2030, it provides the opportunity for the stock to be rerated significantly higher, should management successfully demonstrate that it can deliver the targets it has set.

By launching its new specialty in “Hers,” Hims & Hers will unlock a whole new TAM (total addressable market) with the opportunity for subscriber and revenue acceleration from here onwards. This should be followed by the launch of lab testing and longevity-focused care next year, as management has promised. When investors see faster than expected growth in subscribers and personalized treatment, their confidence in management’s vision and long-term targets will return.

Revisiting my valuation

In the earlier section, I discussed that consensus is heavily underestimating management’s FY 2030 revenue projections, leaving room for Hims & Hers stock to be rerated higher should the company indeed deliver.

One of the other potential tailwinds that Hims & Hers has is the unusually high short float of 35%, which could lead to outsized volatility in the share price when investor confidence returns.

For now, I will be conservative and take forward consensus estimates into account while estimating my price target for Hims & Hers. Over the next three years, consensus estimates point to Hims & Hers generating $3.3B in revenue, growing in the high teens between FY 2026 and FY 2027.

When it comes to profitability, management currently estimates the company to generate an adjusted EBITDA margin of 13.5%. Over the following two years, I will assume that it will be able to unlock at least 100 basis points of operating leverage every year to 15.5% by FY 2027, as it makes progress towards its FY 2030 goals. This will translate to an adjusted EBITDA of $511M by FY 2027, which is equivalent to a present value of $402M when discounted at 8%.

Taking the S&P 500 as a proxy, where its companies grow their earnings at an average rate of 9% every year with a price-to-earnings multiple of 15-18, I believe that Hims & Hers deserves a premium of 2.5 times that of the S&P 500 given the projected growth rate in its earnings. This will translate to a P/E ratio of 45, or a price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Risks and Conclusions

The launch of the new specialty in “Hers” could be the spark that fuels a renewed investor confidence in the company, as it allows it to generate revenues via unlocking TAMs in new specialties that it has planned for in 2026.

In all of this, the macroeconomic environment could potentially turn out to be a headwind, as it can derail consumer spending on the platform.

Having said that, I believe the potential for rerating is significant given the difference in estimates between consensus and management as well as the large short float.

Given the attractive risk-reward at this point, I will upgrade my rating to a Buy with a price target of $80 per share, representing an upside of 30% from its current levels.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
